turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has our opinion this morning in case 18-389, Parker Drilling Management Services v. Newton.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. Respondent Brian Newton worked for petitioner Parker Drilling Management Services on drilling platforms off the California coast. He was paid for his time on duty, but not for his time on standby, during which he could not leave the platform. Newton filed a class action lawsuit alleging that California's minimum wage and overtime laws required Parker to compensate him for his standby time. The parties agreed that Parker's platforms were subject to the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act, which extends federal law to the Outer Continental Shelf. The statute provides that all law on the Outer Continental Shelf is federal law to be administered by federal officials and denies states any interest in the Outer Continental Shelf. Finally, it deems the adjacent states' laws to be federal law to the extent that they are applicable and not inconsistent with other federal law. Interpreting this provision, the District Court concluded that the relevant California laws should be deemed federal law on the Outer Continental Shelf, because the Federal Fair Labor Standards Act left no significant gap in federal law for state law to fill. It thus granted Parker judgment on the pleadings. The Ninth Circuit vacated and remanded. It held that state law is applicable under the statute if it pertains to the subject matter at issue. It also held that the California laws were not inconsistent with federal law, because they were not incompatible with the federal scheme. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we vacate and remand. The statute extends all federal laws to the Outer Continental Shelf and borrows only certain state laws which are declared to be federal law and administered by federal officials. It would therefore make little sense to treat the Outer Continental Shelf as a mere extension of the adjacent state, where state law applies unless it conflicts with federal law. That type of preemption analysis applies only where overlapping dual state and federal jurisdiction makes it necessary to decide which law takes precedence. But the Outer Continental Shelf is not and never was part of a state, so state law has never applied of its own force. Instead, federal law is the only law on the Outer Continental Shelf; therefore, the appropriate question under the statute is whether federal law has already addressed the relevant issue. If so, state law addressing the same issue would necessarily be inconsistent with existing federal law and cannot be adopted as surrogate federal law. Put another way, to the extent federal law applies to a particular issue, state law is inapplicable. Applying these principles here to the extent Newton's claims are premised on the adoption of California law requiring payment for all time that Newton spent on standby, federal law already addresses this issue and Newton's claims therefore fail. Likewise, federal law already provides a minimum wage, so the California minimum wage does not apply on the Outer Continental Shelf. We leave Newton's other claims to be considered by the Lower Courts in the first instance. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we vacate the judgment of the Ninth Circuit and remand for further proceedings. The opinion of the Court is unanimous.
